analysis: Top performers share the same structural pattern: Pareto prefiltering, a cheap single‑point "rect" volume surrogate to rank candidates, a stamped (lazy) max‑heap that only recomputes exact marginal hypervolume for the heap top, caching of computed HVs, and a small bounded local swap refinement. These combine good quality with far fewer exact HV calls. SPESS (Heuristic 1) instead uses an evolutionary sparse subset search (mutation/crossover) which is exploratory but involves many random flips and bookkeeping overhead. Weaker designs tend to (a) overuse Monte‑Carlo with large M×N boolean matrices (huge memory/cost), (b) recompute full set HVs too often (no stamp/cache), or (c) implement nondomination and index mapping with expensive Python loops and repeated array copies. Common avoidable overheads: frequent creation of frozenset/tuple keys, repeated hv(S) recomputation instead of reusing cached hv_S, expensive full-array concatenations per heap pop, and large, fixed Monte‑Carlo sample sizes regardless of N, k, or D. Good ideas to keep: single‑point surrogates for warm starts, stamped lazy updates to minimize exact HV calls, adaptive shortlist sizing (c·k), and small swap passes. Improvements focus on robust caching (set→HV and marginal→stamp), efficient nondominated filtering (vectorized or specialized), adaptive Monte‑Carlo (or bitset chunking) when pg is unavailable, and minimizing Python-level loops and conversions.
experience: Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.
current_reflection: Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.
good_reflections: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates and remove points dominated by the reference. Use lazy greedy with a priority queue storing marginal HV contributions and only recompute when a candidate reaches the top. Cache incremental HV or use per‑point contribution routines instead of full recompute. Combine a cheap surrogate (R2) to shortlist candidates, then refine top ones with exact HV/local swaps. Vectorize mask math and minimize Python loops.

Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto prefilter, stamped lazy greedy, single‑rect warmstarts, adaptive shortlist (c·k), cheap HV cache (sorted tuple/bitmask), pygmo, bitset Monte‑Carlo, vectorized dominance, bounded swaps.
- Advice: Prefilter nondominated candidates, shortlist by cheap rect/R2 surrogate (c·k), drive a stamped max‑heap and recompute exact marginal HV only for heap tops, reuse warmstarts/pygmo or fallback bitset MC, then apply a small bounded local swap pass.
- Avoid: full HV recompute for every candidate, rebuilding arrays/sets/frozensets in loops, unbounded Monte‑Carlo or swap trials, and Python‑level per‑point dominance loops.
- Explanation: Stamps, caches and warmstarts minimize expensive HV calls; vectorization and C libraries shift work off Python, yielding high HV per runtime with predictable cost.
